items.title: Brian Benchoff designed a $15 portable computer.
items.summary: Instead of rooting an Android phone, Brian Benchoff has gone & created his own portable computer that runs Linux &…
items.content_html:
Instead of rooting an Android phone, Brian Benchoff has gone & created his own portable computer that runs Linux & fits in your pocket. The computer currently run buildroot, has no gui & the battery is AAA-sized NiMH batteries.

Buildroot is a Linux version that targets small embedded systems..

For more info.. Please click here to read Brians original post…

items.title: Awesome Arch
items.summary: Arch Linux is a Linux distribution thats lightweight & tries to Keep things Simple. It can be installed as a…
items.content_html: Arch Linux is a Linux distribution thats lightweight & tries to Keep things Simple. It can be installed as a minimal base system & then configured from the ground up by the user. The user can create their own environment, through installing simply only what they may require. Awesome Arch is a list of awesome Arch related projects.

items.title: Installing Arch on termux.
items.summary: Ever wanted to have a portable Arch install that you can take with you whever you go :? Well with…
items.content_html:
Ever wanted to have a portable Arch install that you can take with you whever you go :? Well with the help of an android phone, termux & this bash script you can have just that. It’s not super hard to get set up & it doesn’t take all that long either.

  • To begin you’ll want to go ahead & install termux if you haven’t already done so. Once we have termux installed we can then go ahead & install the base OS.
pkg update && pkg install bsdtar wget proot tergent tmux openssh
  • With the base OS installed you’ll now want to set up storage for termux.
termux-setup-storage
  • When you’ve finished setting up storage we can now download the script & begin setting up Arch.
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/SDRausty/termux-arch/master/setupTermuxArch.bash
  • With the script has downloaded we can then run it.
./setupTermuxArch.bash

This will download and install the base Arch system. The time this will take to finished will vary depending on your device & connection.

  • When everything has finished you’ll be dropped into a root shell for your new Arch install. We can now go ahead & change the username & password for our new install
useradd -mG root -s /bin/bash newuser
passwd newuser

Remember to change newuser to whatever you want your username to be..

  • Once you’ve changed the username & password, we can move on to making arch a little bit more useable. We’ll start by installing wget & then downloading one final script.
sudo pacman -Syyu wget && wget --no-check-certificate  'https://docs.google.com/uc?export=download&id=107Fh0l_p0ItVkUufOhP9to-OU_6KYhPW' -O fresharch.sh
  • When the script has finished downloading, we can make it executable & then run it.
chmod +x fresharch.sh && sudo ./fresharch.sh

If everything goes smoothly & you see no errors on the screen then you should have a fully functional Arch install with yay & AUR support. If you happen to come across any errors, feel free to run through all of the steps again to verify everything. This worked for me whenever I tried it, so it should work for you also….

items.title: Hiding statusbar in tmux.
items.summary: If you are someone like me who occasionally uses tmux, but has no need for the statusbar then this post…
items.content_html:
If you are someone like me who occasionally uses tmux, but has no need for the statusbar then this post is for you. All you need to do is add the line below into your ~/.tmux.conf.

set -g status off

There was no tmux.conf by default on my system so I had to create it. You’ll probably need to do the same..

Tmux with statusbar showing..

Tmux with statusbar hidden..

items.title: Creating a simple blog with markdown & bash.
items.summary: Using only markdown, bash & a converter we can create a super simple blog. To begin you’ll want to go…
items.content_html:
Using only markdown, bash & a converter we can create a super simple blog. To begin you’ll want to go ahead & install some sort of markdown converter that works inside your terminal. For this I use discount & on my arch system I can easily install it with pacman -S discount.

I’ll be using discount for this post, so make sure to change any commands as needed for whatever converter you choose to use..

So before we begin you’ll want to create a blog directory inside your home folder.

mkdir ~/blog

You can create this directory anywhere, but for me my home folder makes sense..

To get started lets create a template.html & add the lines <!-- insert here --> in the middle.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<title>Startpage..</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="Style.css">
<meta charset="utf-8">
<base target="_blank">
</head>
<body>
<div class="hello">

<!-- insert here -->

</div>
</body>
</html>

The insert here park is where our post will be inserted later..

With the .html created we can move onto the .css. This is were we will theme our blog & you can really add anything you wish. For this post I’ve just gone & centered everything in the middle of the page.

.hello {
  position: fixed;
  top: 50%;
  left: 50%;
  width: 100%;
  transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
  font-family: Ubuntu;
  font-size: 22px;
  font-weight: 300;
  text-align: center;
}

You can save this file with whatever name you choose, as long as you remember to change the link in the .html to match..

Once you have the directory & html/css created, we can then move on to creating our script. This first piece of code is our shebang & it’s used at the start of any script to tell the operating system which interpreter to use to parse the rest of the file.

#!/usr/bin/env bash

This next part will be our converter. This will convert all our .md files to .html.

for i in `ls *.md`; do
  markdown $i > ~/blog/`basename $i .md`.html;
done

Now that we have all of our files converted we can start editing our html. What we need to do is surround all our new html files with <div class='entry'> and output everything into a file called entries.html.

ls ~/blog/* | xargs -n 1 awk 'BEGIN {print "<div class=\"entry\">" } {print $0} END {print "</div>"}' > entries.html

We’re almost done now as this is the final command for our script. This command will insert everything in entries.html into your template.html file and output the result into a new file, called index.html.

sed -e '/<!-- insert here/r entries.html' template.html > index.html

With that last command added to our script we can go ahead & save it, then make it executable.

chmod +x bashblog.sh

That’s our script created, so now we can test it out by creating a few simple blog posts in markdown & then run the script. If your script fails at any point, make sure to check all the paths entered in the script are correct & that you have the correct command if using a different converter.

items.title: Chaning the background color of image tabs in firefox.
items.summary: This is how an open image in firefox usually looks. By simply adding the following code to your userContent.css, you…
items.content_html:
This is how an open image in firefox usually looks.

By simply adding the following code to your userContent.css, you can change the color to whatever you want. It’s super simple….

/* Imagetab */
@-moz-document regexp("((.*\\.(png|jpg|jpeg|gif|tiff|bmp|ico|PNG|JPG|JPEG|GIF|TIFF|BMP|ICO))|(^data:image/.*)|(.*/attachment\.php[?]attachmentid=[0-9]+.*)|(about:logo))") {

  /* site background */
  html:not([id]):not([class]):not([lang]):not([dir]):not([xmlns]) >
  body:not([id]):not([class]):not([lang]):not([dir]):not([style]):not([title]):not([bgcolor]):not([text]):not([link]):not([onload]) {
    background-image: none !important; /* remove noisy background */
    background-color: #272B35 !important; /* change background color */
  }

  /* transparent image background */
  img.decoded {
    background-image: none !important; /* remove noisy background */
    background-color: #272B35 !important; /* change background color */
  }

  /* transparent image background when hovering (checkerboard) */
  img.decoded:hover {
    background-image: url('data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhFAAUAIABAP///wAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAAUABQAAAIojI+gy+jBInhJLnrsxVBP7n1YyHVaSYKhh7Lq+VotPLo1TaW3HEtlAQA7') !important;
    background-color: #272B35 !important; /* change background color (here: color of dark squares) */
  }
}

items.title: Downloading Age Restricted Videos With youtube-dl.
items.summary: A while back youtube.com started adding age restrictions to Videos & trying to download some of these videos with youtube-dl,…
items.content_html:
A while back youtube.com started adding age restrictions to Videos & trying to download some of these videos with youtube-dl, simply resulted in people receiving the following error message.

WARNING: Unable to download video info webpage: HTTP Error 410: Gone
ERROR: Sign in to confirm your age
This video may be inappropriate for some users.

You still want to download the video right :? The simple solution is to use a browser extension to download your youtube cookies & then pass said cookies to youtube-dl with the --cookies flag. This was all pretty straight forward & I was able to download the age restricted video in no time. Simply follow the steps below if you’d like to be able to do the same.

  • Step.1

    Install a browser extension to download your cookies from youtube. I’m running firefox so I installed “this extension” & saved my cookies to my downloads folder.

  • Step.2

    Add the --cookies flag to your youtube-dl command in the terminal & follow it with the location of your cookies.txt file.

youtube-dl -f bestvideo+bestaudio --cookies /path/to/youtube-cookies.txt $youtubeurl

Thats all there is to it. Run the command & your video should download as normal without giving you any age restriction errors.

items.title: Identifying an operating system using only the ping command.
items.summary: You can identify which operating system is running simply by pinging it. All you need to do is ping localhost…
items.content_html:
You can identify which operating system is running simply by pinging it. All you need to do is ping localhost & read the TTL value. This should work on almost any operating system that has access to ping from a terminal. Obviously theres way better ways to find the currently running operating system, but hey this is still cool nonetheless. Simply run the ping command below in a terminal & then compare the TTL value with the table below to determine the opertaing system.

ping -c5 localhost
OS VERSION PROTOCOL TTL
AIX TCP 60
AIX UDP 30
Android 3.2.1 TCP and ICMP 64
Android 5.1.1 TCP and ICMP 64
AIX 3.2, 4.1 ICMP 255
BSDI BSD/OS 3.1 and 4.0 ICMP 255
Compa Tru64 v5.0 ICMP 64
Cisco ICMP 254
DEC Pathworks V5 TCP and UDP 30
Foundry ICMP 64
FreeBSD 2.1R TCP and UDP 64
FreeBSD 3.4, 4.0 ICMP 255
FreeBSD 5 ICMP 64
HP-UX 9.0x TCP and UDP 30
HP-UX 10.01 TCP and UDP 64
HP-UX 10.2 ICMP 255
HP-UX 11 ICMP 255
HP-UX 11 TCP 64
Irix 5.3 TCP and UDP 60
Irix 6.x TCP and UDP 60
Irix 6.5.3, 6.5.8 ICMP 255
juniper ICMP 64
MPE/IX (HP) ICMP 200
Linux 2.0.x kernel ICMP 64
Linux 2.2.14 kernel ICMP 255
Linux 2.4 kernel ICMP 255
Linux Red Hat 9 ICMP and TCP 64
MacOS/MacTCP 2.0.x TCP and UDP 60
MacOS/MacTCP X (10.5.6) ICMP/TCP/UDP 64
NetBSD ICMP 255
Netgear FVG318 ICMP and UDP 64
OpenBSD 2.6 & 2.7 ICMP 255
OpenVMS 07.01.2002 ICMP 255
OS/2 TCP/IP 3.0 64
OSF/1 V3.2A TCP 60
OSF/1 V3.2A UDP 30
Solaris 2.5.1, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8 ICMP 255
Solaris 2.8 TCP 64
Stratus TCP_OS ICMP 255
Stratus TCP_OS (14.2-) TCP and UDP 30
Stratus TCP_OS (14.3+) TCP and UDP 64
Stratus STCP ICMP/TCP/UDP 60
SunOS 4.1.3/4.1.4 TCP and UDP 60
SunOS 5.7 ICMP and TCP 255
Ultrix V4.1/V4.2A TCP 60
Ultrix V4.1/V4.2A UDP 30
Ultrix V4.2 – 4.5 ICMP 255
VMS/Multinet TCP and UDP 64
VMS/TCPware TCP 60
VMS/TCPware UDP 64
VMS/Wollongong 1.1.1.1 TCP 128
VMS/Wollongong 1.1.1.1 UDP 30
VMS/UCX TCP and UDP 128
Windows for Workgroups TCP and UDP 32
Windows 95 TCP and UDP 32
Windows 98 ICMP 32
Windows 98, 98 SE ICMP 128
Windows 98 TCP 128
Windows NT 3.51 TCP and UDP 32
Windows NT 4.0 TCP and UDP 128
Windows NT 4.0 SP5- 32
Windows NT 4.0 SP6+ 128
Windows NT 4 WRKS SP 3, SP 6a ICMP 128
Windows NT 4 Server SP4 ICMP 128
Windows ME ICMP 128
Windows 2000 pro ICMP/TCP/UDP 128
Windows 2000 family ICMP 128
Windows Server 2003 128
Windows XP ICMP/TCP/UDP 128
Windows Vista ICMP/TCP/UDP 128
Windows 7 ICMP/TCP/UDP 128
Windows Server 2008 ICMP/TCP/UDP 128
Windows 10 ICMP/TCP/UDP 128

items.title: Using imagemagick as an image viewer.
items.summary: Did you know that you could use imagemagick as an image viewer :? Well I didn’t until recently & it’s…
items.content_html:
Did you know that you could use imagemagick as an image viewer :? Well I didn’t until recently & it’s super easy to do so. Simply create & save the super small bash script below & call it from the terminal or from a custom action within your file manager. You’ll just need to change 1920x1200 to whatever your screen resolution is.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Open images with imagemagick....

display -resize 1920x1200 "$1"

I saved the script to /usr/bin/image so all I have to do now is run image $/path/to/image.jpeg when in a terminal.

items.title: Installing imagemagick on debian.
items.summary: Imagemagick is one heck of an awesome program & you never know when it may just come in handy. The…
items.content_html:
Imagemagick is one heck of an awesome program & you never know when it may just come in handy. The only problem is that if you happen to run debian you’ll find that it’s not included within the repos, therefore you’ll need to compile it from source. Never worry.. Compiling imagemagick from source isn’t all that compilicated & I’m going to show you how to do just that right now.

Step.1

Prepare your system by opening up a terminal & installing the build-essential package that includes things like make & gcc.

sudo apt update && sudo apt-get install build-essential

Step.2

In the same terminal window use wget to download the latest source code archive directly from the imagemagick website.

wget https://www.imagemagick.org/download/ImageMagick.tar.gz

Step.3

Once the download has finished we can then extract it’s contents.

tar xvzf ImageMagick.tar.gz

Step.4

With everything extracted we can now start compiling the source code. You’ll first want to configure the ImageMagick make script by running the ./configure command to configure compiler environment.

./configure

Once configure has finished, we can then use the make command to start the compiling.

make

Look out for any errors that may appear within the terminal window & ddg them to search for the fix..

Step.5

When compiling finishes we can go & install the imagemagick binary file using make install command.

sudo make install

Step.6

Imagemagick should now be installed on your debian system. You can launch it from the terminal by running the display command & display version info by running magick -version.
